title: A Novel Framework for Spatiotemporal Groundwater Pumping Process Estimation Based on Data-Driven Approaches

research.question: Can pumping locations, operating times, rates, and total withdrawals be recovered from dense spatiotemporal groundwater-head observations without prior pumping records?
research.system: A groundwater system whose observed heads contain localized intermittent pumping signals mixed with broader recharge, precipitation, trend, and boundary responses.
research.inputData: First-differenced groundwater heads from a synthetic 20 km by 20 km homogeneous unconfined MODFLOW aquifer with four intermittent wells, constant-head and no-flow boundaries, and spatially uniform infiltration driven by an observed Taiwan rainfall series.
research.method: Bayesian maximum-entropy interpolation supports uneven observations; first differencing removes trend; rotated EOFs localize pumping-like spatial modes; HHT separates contaminated temporal modes; restored head changes are scaled by storage coefficient into pumping-rate fields.
research.benchmark: The synthetic MODFLOW model is checked against the Theis solution with Jacob correction, and estimated space-time pumping is compared with four designed pumping series and amounts.
research.uncertainty: The paper diagnoses delayed hydraulic response, constant-head recharge, no-flow boundary amplification, storage-coefficient uncertainty, observation spacing, temporal sampling, and pumping-impact radius; it does not report probabilistic intervals.
research.conclusion: The framework recovers pumping-centered spatial patterns and temporal rates with a 6.6% relative error in total pumping for the synthetic case, provided head observations are sufficiently dense and frequent relative to pumping.

narrative.throughline: Pumping leaves a local high-frequency signature in groundwater heads, and separating that signature in space before cleaning it in time turns missing withdrawal records into an estimable process.
narrative.audienceStartingPoint: The audience understands that pumping lowers groundwater levels but that rainfall, recharge, and boundaries also move the same observations.
narrative.stakes: Unknown withdrawals increase groundwater-balance uncertainty and weaken management where permit or measurement systems are incomplete.
narrative.resolution: Spatial EOF localization finds candidate wells, HHT cleans their temporal coefficients, and storage-scaled head changes reconstruct pumping rates across space and time.

background.premise: Understanding the spatial and temporal distribution of pumping activities is necessary for effective water-resource management.
background.visibleEvidence: A groundwater-head map shows four unseen pumping wells leaving moving cones of depression.
background.whyNow: The scientific object is not a single head curve but an unrecorded withdrawal process distributed over a managed aquifer.
background.boundary: The paper estimates pumping from head observations rather than replacing regulatory metering where direct measurements exist.
background.sourceExcerpt: temporal distribution of pumping activities is crucial for effective water
background.visualObject: Aquifer map with observation wells and hidden pumping cones
background.motionPurpose: Pulse the hidden cones so their space-time signature becomes the object to recover.
background.stepDetail: Show observation wells; lower head at hidden sources; propagate cones; remove the pumping labels while retaining head changes.
background.transitionBridge: The unlabeled head field becomes a data matrix.

background.premise: Accurate pumping records are often unavailable where groundwater-withdrawal permit systems are inefficient.
background.visibleEvidence: A pumping ledger develops blank spatial and temporal entries while head sensors continue recording.
background.whyNow: The missing records explain why the method must infer pumping from an indirect but abundant observable.
background.boundary: The proposed approach is most relevant where space-time head observations are relatively abundant.
background.sourceExcerpt: However, obtaining accurate pumping records is often challenging, particularly in
background.visualObject: Incomplete withdrawal ledger beside a complete groundwater-head sensor network
background.motionPurpose: Fade the missing ledger entries while leaving the observation network active.
background.stepDetail: Open the withdrawal ledger; blank its location and rate fields; illuminate continuous head records; connect observations to inference.
background.transitionBridge: The sensor traces overlap as mixed natural and pumping signals.

background.premise: Natural and anthropogenic forcings including pumping, precipitation, tides, and evapotranspiration exert different impacts on groundwater-head variation.
background.visibleEvidence: Pumping, precipitation, tidal, and evapotranspiration signals coexist in one groundwater-head record.
background.whyNow: The viewer must see why a head change alone cannot be attributed to pumping.
background.boundary: The synthetic test emphasizes direct infiltration and four intermittent pumping processes rather than every possible forcing.
background.locator: PDF page 2, Section 1 Introduction, final paragraphs
background.sourceExcerpt: System forcings driven by natural or anthropogenic processes may
background.visualObject: Mixed head trace linked to four source-forcing symbols
background.motionPurpose: Show distinct forcing paths converging on the same observed head record.
background.stepDetail: Introduce pumping, precipitation, tide, and evapotranspiration symbols; connect each to the observed head trace; retain the mixed observation.
background.transitionBridge: The mixed observations are interpolated onto a common space-time support.

methods.input: A homogeneous unconfined aquifer with source-defined hydraulic properties boundaries infiltration and four designed intermittent wells
methods.output: MODFLOW groundwater-head observations containing the designed forcings
methods.validity: The paper constructs a two-dimensional finite-difference MODFLOW model from modified Choushui River alluvial-fan properties.
methods.locator: PDF page 4, Section 3.1 Synthetic aquifer and head observations; Figure 1
methods.sourceExcerpt: For the validation of the method, we developed a two-dimensional
methods.visualObject: MODFLOW aquifer grid with four intermittent wells and source-defined boundaries
methods.motionPurpose: Create the synthetic head field that owns the later designed pumping comparison.
methods.stepDetail: Run the source-defined finite-difference aquifer with its designed wells infiltration and boundary conditions.
methods.transitionBridge: The numerical head response is held against the Theis-Jacob benchmark in M02.

methods.input: MODFLOW drawdown response and the Theis solution with Jacob correction
methods.output: Verified numerical aquifer response for subsequent pumping recovery
methods.validity: The paper explicitly verifies its numerical model by comparison with the Theis solution with Jacob correction.
methods.locator: PDF page 4, Section 3.1 Synthetic aquifer and head observations
methods.sourceExcerpt: has been verified by comparing it with the Theis solution with Jacob’s
methods.visualObject: MODFLOW and Theis-Jacob drawdown traces on one axis
methods.motionPurpose: Give the synthetic forward model an explicit analytical benchmark owner.
methods.stepDetail: Overlay the numerical response on the Theis-Jacob reference and retain the verified MODFLOW field.
methods.transitionBridge: The verified but unevenly sampled head observations enter interpolation in M03.

methods.input: Unevenly sampled groundwater-head observations
methods.output: A regular spatiotemporal head field
methods.validity: Bayesian maximum entropy accounts for the geometry of clustered observations before EOF decomposition.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.2 Empirical orthogonal function analysis
methods.sourceExcerpt: groundwater heads using a geostatistical method, i.e., the Bayesian maximum
methods.visualObject: Irregular observation dots filling into a regular space-time head lattice
methods.motionPurpose: Create common spatial support without allowing clustered wells to dominate the covariance structure.
methods.stepDetail: Place uneven observations; estimate the head between them; fill the regular lattice; preserve observation anchors.
methods.transitionBridge: Each interpolated lattice cell becomes the original head series differenced in M04.

methods.input: Original groundwater-head time series at each location
methods.output: First-differenced stationary high-frequency head changes
methods.validity: Consecutive differencing removes slower level and seasonal trends that can mask local pumping variability.
methods.locator: PDF page 2, Section 2.1 Trend removal, Equation 1
methods.sourceExcerpt: to time series of groundwater head observations to retrieve stationary
methods.visualObject: Sloping head traces converting into zero-centered increments
methods.motionPurpose: Subtract consecutive observations so high-frequency pumping changes remain while the slow trend disappears.
methods.stepDetail: Pair adjacent head values; form their difference; repeat through time; align the resulting increment series around zero.
methods.transitionBridge: The differenced series stack into the space-time matrix decomposed in M05.

methods.input: First-differenced spatiotemporal head matrix
methods.output: Orthogonal spatial patterns with temporal expansion coefficients
methods.validity: Singular-value decomposition orders modes by the variance they explain in the differenced field.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.2 Empirical orthogonal function analysis, Equations 2-4
methods.sourceExcerpt: The major spatiotemporal patterns of the differenced series
methods.visualObject: Space-time matrix separating into EOF maps and EC time series
methods.motionPurpose: Split the mixed field into paired spatial and temporal objects that can be inspected independently.
methods.stepDetail: Display the matrix; factor it with SVD; unfold columns as spatial EOFs; attach each temporal EC.
methods.transitionBridge: The EOF results retain their loading and EC ownership through the stated rescaling in M06.

methods.input: Raw EOF loading patterns
methods.output: Source-stated rescaled EOF representation prepared for Varimax rotation
methods.validity: The paper states that EOF results are rescaled but supplies no numeric rescaling factors for the visual narrative.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.2 Empirical orthogonal function analysis
methods.sourceExcerpt: Furthermore, the EOF results are rescaled and rotated
methods.visualObject: EOF loading maps retained in the paper's stated rescaled representation
methods.motionPurpose: Represent the stated preprocessing without inventing factor values.
methods.stepDetail: Rescale the EOF results at the source-stated level and display no numeric factor or unsupported coefficient.
methods.transitionBridge: The source-stated rescaled representation enters Varimax rotation in M07.

methods.input: Rescaled orthogonal EOF loadings
methods.output: Varimax-rotated localized spatial patterns
methods.validity: Orthogonal Varimax rotation pushes loadings toward zero or large signed values, simplifying the EOF structure.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.2 Empirical orthogonal function analysis
methods.sourceExcerpt: matrix is applied to EOF rotation to simplify the EOF structure, pushing
methods.visualObject: Diffuse EOF loading clouds rotating into compact spatial lobes
methods.motionPurpose: Rotate the modal basis until localized forcing footprints become legible.
methods.stepDetail: Show diffuse loadings; rotate the orthogonal axes; concentrate large loadings; retain orthogonality.
methods.transitionBridge: The rotated maps enter the source's relative-localization classification in M08.

methods.input: Rotated EOF spatial patterns
methods.output: Pumping-like EOF modes and their EC time series
methods.validity: In the synthetic system, direct infiltration has a broad footprint while each pumping station has a relatively local footprint.
methods.locator: PDF page 5, Section 3.2 Results, Figure 3
methods.sourceExcerpt: temporal result, we consider the EOF results with relatively spatially-localized
methods.visualObject: Broad recharge EOF separated from compact pumping-centered EOF spots
methods.motionPurpose: Use the source's relative spatial-localization judgment to retain pumping-associated candidates.
methods.stepDetail: Compare the relative spatial extent of rotated patterns and retain the source-identified EOF2 through EOF9 as pumping-associated candidates.
methods.transitionBridge: Each source-identified pumping-like EC expands into intrinsic mode functions in M09.

methods.input: One pumping-like EC time series
methods.output: Intrinsic mode functions and a residual trend
methods.validity: Empirical mode decomposition retrieves IMFs by recursive sifting of the pumping-like EC.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.3 Hilbert-Huang transform analysis, Equation 5
methods.sourceExcerpt: retrieved by a recursive sifting process using the empirical mode
methods.visualObject: One EC trace peeling into stacked intrinsic-mode oscillations
methods.motionPurpose: Separate the pumping-like EC into intrinsic oscillatory modes and a residual trend.
methods.stepDetail: Envelope the local extrema; remove the envelope mean; repeat sifting; stack the resulting IMFs.
methods.transitionBridge: Each IMF becomes the real component of the analytic signal formed in M10.

methods.input: One intrinsic mode function
methods.output: Complex analytic signal for the IMF
methods.validity: The source forms each analytic signal from the IMF and its Hilbert transform.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.3 Hilbert-Huang transform analysis, paragraph following Equation 5
methods.sourceExcerpt: is a well-defined Hilbert spectrum, and its complex form or analytic
methods.visualObject: Real IMF paired with its Hilbert component as one analytic signal
methods.motionPurpose: Own the Hilbert transformation before deriving either temporal attribute.
methods.stepDetail: Pair the IMF with its Hilbert transform and retain the resulting analytic signal.
methods.transitionBridge: The analytic-signal magnitude becomes instantaneous amplitude in M11.

methods.input: Complex analytic signal for one IMF
methods.output: Time-varying IMF amplitude series
methods.validity: The HHT representation uses analytic-signal amplitude as one source-defined temporal attribute.
methods.locator: PDF page 7, Figure 5d
methods.sourceExcerpt: IMF1-associated time-varying (d) instantaneous amplitude
methods.visualObject: Analytic-signal magnitude traced through time
methods.motionPurpose: Give instantaneous amplitude its own calculated quantity and owner.
methods.stepDetail: Calculate the analytic-signal magnitude at each time and retain it beside the IMF.
methods.transitionBridge: The same analytic signal supplies instantaneous frequency in M12.

methods.input: Complex analytic signal for one IMF
methods.output: Time-varying IMF frequency series
methods.validity: Figure 5 reports the IMF-associated instantaneous frequency used by the contamination criteria.
methods.locator: PDF page 7, Figure 5e
methods.sourceExcerpt: (e) frequency series, and (f)
methods.visualObject: Analytic-signal phase rate traced through time
methods.motionPurpose: Give instantaneous frequency its own calculated quantity and owner.
methods.stepDetail: Calculate the analytic-signal phase rate at each time and retain it beside the amplitude series.
methods.transitionBridge: The owned amplitude and frequency series combine into the product in M13.

methods.input: Instantaneous amplitude and instantaneous frequency for one IMF
methods.output: Frequency-amplitude product series
methods.validity: The source uses the product as an indicator correlated with precipitation contamination.
methods.locator: PDF page 5, Section 3.2; PDF page 7, Figure 5f
methods.sourceExcerpt: and the product of the frequency and amplitude series has a strong
methods.visualObject: Amplitude and frequency traces multiplying pointwise into a product trace
methods.motionPurpose: Own the product before applying the outlier criteria.
methods.stepDetail: Multiply the synchronized amplitude and frequency values at each time.
methods.transitionBridge: The frequency series and owned product enter the outlier classification in M14.

methods.input: IMF frequency and frequency-amplitude product series
methods.output: Outlier times associated with non-pumping contamination
methods.validity: Equation 6 applies source-defined median and standard-deviation criteria; Figure 5 marks identified outliers in red.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.3, Equation 6; PDF page 7, Figure 5
methods.sourceExcerpt: the marked red points denote identified outliers
methods.visualObject: Red outlier marks on the frequency and product traces
methods.motionPurpose: Give contamination classification its own visible output.
methods.stepDetail: Apply the two source-defined criteria and mark only the times they classify as outliers.
methods.transitionBridge: The owned outlier times transfer to the pumping-like EC for removal in M15.

methods.input: Pumping-like EC with outlier times from all contaminated IMFs
methods.output: Bounded gaps in the pumping-like EC
methods.validity: At each iteration the source removes EC values at times contaminated in any IMF.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.3, paragraph following Equation 6
methods.sourceExcerpt: the EC is removed at times when the contaminated signals existing in any of the IMFs are contaminated
methods.visualObject: Outlier-aligned EC samples lifting away as bounded gaps
methods.motionPurpose: Own removal separately from reconstruction and stopping evaluation.
methods.stepDetail: Project all IMF outlier times onto the EC and remove only those values.
methods.transitionBridge: The bounded EC gaps become interpolation intervals in M16.

methods.input: Pumping-like EC with bounded gaps
methods.output: Reconstructed pumping-like EC for the current iteration
methods.validity: The source reconstructs removed values with conventional time-series interpolation and uses mirror extension to preserve series length.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.3, paragraph following Equation 6
methods.sourceExcerpt: then reconstructed using a conventional time-series interpolation method.
methods.visualObject: Interpolated segments reconnecting the bounded EC gaps
methods.motionPurpose: Own temporal reconstruction separately from contamination removal.
methods.stepDetail: Interpolate each bounded gap and apply the source-stated mirror extension at the iteration boundary.
methods.transitionBridge: The reconstructed EC returns to both source criteria for evaluation in M17.

methods.input: Reconstructed EC and both Equation 6 contamination criteria
methods.output: A denoised EC when both criteria are satisfied
methods.validity: The source performs the two criteria recursively and stops when both the frequency and product tests are satisfied.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.3, paragraph following Equation 6
methods.sourceExcerpt: two criterion in Eq. (6) are performed recursively until
methods.visualObject: Two criterion indicators settling into their satisfied states beside the denoised EC
methods.motionPurpose: Give the recursive stopping decision an explicit evaluation owner.
methods.stepDetail: Recompute both criterion states and retain the EC only when each is satisfied.
methods.transitionBridge: The evaluated denoised EC supplies its extrema to pumping-time identification in M18.

methods.input: Denoised pumping-like EC
methods.output: Pumping-on and pumping-off timestamps
methods.validity: For the restored pumping signature, local maxima and minima correspond to turning the pumping station on and off.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.3 Hilbert-Huang transform analysis, paragraph preceding Equation 7
methods.sourceExcerpt: turning on and off the pumping stations, respectively. On the basis
methods.visualObject: EC extrema snapping to on-off switch markers
methods.motionPurpose: Translate extrema in the head-change signature into discrete operating intervals.
methods.stepDetail: Find a local maximum; mark pump-on; follow the interval; find a local minimum; mark pump-off.
methods.transitionBridge: The owned operating intervals remain fixed while differenced heads are back-transformed in M19.

methods.input: Denoised first-differenced EC and its pumping-on and pumping-off intervals
methods.output: Actual pumping-associated head variation over each operating interval
methods.validity: Because EOF analysis operates on first differences the source requires a back transformation to recover actual head variation.
methods.locator: PDF page 5, Section 3.2; PDF page 8, Figure 7
methods.sourceExcerpt: a back-transform is required to obtain the actual head variation
methods.visualObject: First-differenced EC increments accumulating into restored head variation
methods.motionPurpose: Give the back transformation its own state before rate conversion.
methods.stepDetail: Accumulate the denoised differenced values within each owned operating interval to restore head variation.
methods.transitionBridge: The restored interval head variation enters the storage-based rate calculation in M20.

methods.input: Restored interval head variation EOF spatial loading storage coefficient and interval duration
methods.output: Mode-specific space-time pumping-rate field
methods.validity: Equation 7 maps restored head change through the EOF spatial pattern and storage coefficient over the operating duration.
methods.locator: PDF pages 4-6, Equation 7 and Section 3.2
methods.sourceExcerpt: the estimated rate in space–time can be obtained by using
methods.visualObject: Restored head-change slab becoming a mode-specific pumping-rate field
methods.motionPurpose: Own the physical head-to-rate conversion without combining it with back transformation.
methods.stepDetail: Apply the source Equation 7 terms to the restored head change and retain the mode-specific rate field.
methods.transitionBridge: All owned mode-specific rate fields align for aggregation in M21.

methods.input: Mode-specific pumping-rate fields
methods.output: Total spatiotemporal pumping-rate distribution
methods.validity: EOF additivity permits reconstruction across all identified pumping-associated modes.
methods.locator: PDF page 4, Section 2.3, paragraph following Equation 7
methods.sourceExcerpt: ing all identified pump-induced features across space and time, that is,
methods.visualObject: Localized rate patches and time series merging into one pumping map
methods.motionPurpose: Recombine separated pumping modes without restoring the broad precipitation modes.
methods.stepDetail: Align mode-specific fields; add their rates at each space-time cell; retain four pumping-centered totals.
methods.transitionBridge: The reconstructed total field aligns with the designed MODFLOW pumping series for comparison in M22.

methods.input: Designed MODFLOW pumping amounts and estimated amounts for four pumping areas
methods.output: Per-well and total designed-versus-estimated pumping amounts
methods.validity: Table 1 reports exact and estimated amounts for every designed well and their total.
methods.locator: PDF page 6, Section 3.2 and Table 1; PDF page 8, Figure 8
methods.sourceExcerpt: Exact and estimated pumping amounts for four pumping wells and the total pumping amounts.
methods.visualObject: Four designed and estimated pumping time-series pairs with total amount columns
methods.motionPurpose: Give the designed-versus-estimated comparison a dedicated owner.
methods.stepDetail: Pair each estimated pumping field with its designed well series and align their total amounts from Table 1.
methods.transitionBridge: The owned amount pairs enter absolute and relative error evaluation in M23.

methods.input: Per-well and total designed-versus-estimated pumping amounts
methods.output: Table 1 absolute and relative errors including 6.6 percent total relative error
methods.validity: The source defines AE and RE in Table 1 and reports a total relative error of 6.6 percent for the synthetic case.
methods.locator: PDF page 6, Section 3.2 and Table 1
methods.sourceExcerpt: with a total relative error of 6.6%.
methods.visualObject: AE and RE markers attached to each owned amount pair with the total row emphasized
methods.motionPurpose: Assign the final quantitative evaluation to a distinct scene owner.
methods.stepDetail: Calculate the table-owned AE and RE entries and retain the reported 6.6 percent total result.
methods.transitionBridge: The evaluated pumping field returns to the original aquifer map for the source-bounded conclusion.

symbols.meaning: groundwater head at spatial position s and time t
symbols.transition: Each interpolated head series becomes the input to first differencing.
The differenced field stacks into the EOF data matrix.

symbols.visualObject: paired spatial loading map and expansion-coefficient series
symbols.meaning: the ith EOF spatial mode and its temporal expansion coefficient
symbols.transition: The pair remains linked through rescaling rotation localization and temporal cleaning.

symbols.meaning: instantaneous amplitude of the ith IMF analytic signal
symbols.transition: The amplitude trace aligns pointwise with frequency for the product calculation.

symbols.meaning: instantaneous frequency of the ith IMF
symbols.transition: The frequency trace aligns pointwise with amplitude for the product calculation.

symbols.meaning: estimated space-time pumping rate associated with the ith EOF feature
symbols.transition: All mode-specific rate fields align for aggregation.

symbols.meaning: absolute and relative errors of estimated pumping amount
symbols.transition: The error markers remain attached to their designed-versus-estimated amount pairs.

return.visualObject: The synthetic aquifer map with four designed wells, pumping-centered estimated-rate patches, and paired designed-versus-estimated time series
return.claim: The EOF-HHT framework can recover localized pumping patterns and useful pumping-rate histories from dense groundwater-head observations in the tested synthetic aquifer.
return.claimBoundary: Accuracy depends on monitoring frequency and spacing, storage-coefficient knowledge, pumping-impact radius, hydraulic delay, and boundary effects; the result is a synthetic validation rather than a field demonstration with independently metered withdrawals.
return.locator: PDF page 6, Section 3.2 Results, Table 1; PDF pages 8-10, Discussion and Conclusions
return.sourceExcerpt: Compared to the designed pumping data, our results demonstrate that our proposed method is able to produce accurate pumping estimates

visual.primaryObject: Spatiotemporal groundwater-head field containing hidden pumping signatures
visual.accentObject: Localized EOF footprints and denoised EC extrema
visual.measuredQuantity: Groundwater head change and inferred pumping rate
visual.geometryNotes: Keep the aquifer map and observation network persistent; transform head traces into first differences, the space-time matrix into EOF maps and ECs, localized ECs into IMF stacks, cleaned extrema into operating intervals, and restored head-volume changes into pumping-rate patches.
